1: vaashuv vaessa et einai vaere vehineh megillah ufah: 'Again I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, a flying scroll!' The sixth vision: the flying scroll. Megillah (H4039 — scroll, book roll) + ufah (H5774 — flying) — an enormous scroll flying through the air, twenty cubits long by ten cubits wide. The scroll's dimensions (20 × 10 cubits) are exactly the dimensions of the porch/doorway of Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6:3), suggesting the scroll is a proclamation emanating from the sanctuary. The flying scroll is a visual theophany of the Torah's curse-provisions activated over the land.
2: vayomer elai mah atta ro'eh vaomar ani ro'eh megillah ufah orkah esrim be'amah verachbah eser be'amah: 'And he said to me, What do you see? I answered, I see a flying scroll. Its length is twenty cubits, and its width ten cubits.' The large dimensions (20 × 10 = 200 square cubits) make the scroll visible to all — it is not a private document but a public proclamation. The measurement is given explicitly because the size is significant: this is the most public possible announcement of divine judgment on covenant violators.
3: vayomer elai zot ha'alah hayotzeit al penei kol ha'aretz ki kol ganav mikkezeh kamo nichah vekhol hanishba sheqer mikkezeh kamo nichah: 'And he said to me, This is the curse (ha'alah) that goes out over the face of the whole land. For everyone who steals shall be cleaned out according to what is on one side, and everyone who swears falsely shall be cleaned out according to what is on the other side.' The scroll contains two curse-texts: against stealing (ganav) and against false swearing (nishba sheqer) — representative of the Decalogue's commandments against humans (stealing, the eighth) and against God (false oaths using his name, the third). These two represent the two tables of the law: toward neighbor and toward God. The curse-scroll is the covenant's punitive mechanism activated.
4: hotzetiv neum YHWH tzvaot uvaat el beit haganav veal beit hanishba veshmi sheqer vellanah betokhh beito ukhillathu ve'et etsav ve'et avanav: 'I will send it out, declares the LORD of hosts, and it shall enter the house of the thief, and the house of him who swears falsely by my name. And it shall remain in his house and consume it, both timber and stones.' The curse enters the covenant-breaker's house and remains (vellanah, to lodge/pass the night) — a persistent presence of judgment that destroys even the physical structure: timber and stones. The image of a self-executing divine curse that physically destroys the violator's house echoes Deuteronomy 28:15-68 (covenant curses) and Habakkuk 2:11 (the stone in the wall cries out).
5: vayetze hamalakh haddover bi vayomer elai sa na et eineicha ure'ah mah hayotzeit hazot: 'Then the angel who talked with me came forward and said to me, Lift your eyes and see what this is that is going out.' The seventh vision: the woman in the basket (ephah). The shift from the curse-scroll to the container of wickedness continues the theme of sin's judgment and removal from the land.
6: vaomar mah hi vayomer zot ha'eifah hayotzeit vayomer zot einam bekhol ha'aretz: 'I said, What is it? And he said, This is the basket that is going out. And he said, This is their iniquity in all the land.' The eifah (H374 — ephah, a standard unit of measurement, about 22 liters) here contains einam (their iniquity, H5771 — the accumulated evil of the land). The ephah-as-wickedness-container is a commercial measure turned into a symbol: the trading and commerce of the land (eiphas were used for measuring grain in trade) has become the vessel of wickedness. The measurement-metaphor implies: sin has been carefully measured out and will be precisely dealt with.
7: vehineh kikkar oferet nissa uhineh ishah achat yoshevet betokh ha'eifah: 'And behold, the leaden cover was lifted, and there was a woman sitting in the basket!' A woman (ishah) sitting in the ephah, confined by a lead cover (kikkar oferet — a circular piece of lead). The woman is identified in v8 as Wickedness (harishab, the wicked one). The feminine figure of personified wickedness may reflect the goddess-worship (Asherah, Astarte) that represented one of Israel's most persistent covenant violations. She is contained, covered, and in the process of being transported away from the land.
8: vayomer zot harishab vayashlech otah el tokh ha'eifah vayashlech et even ha'oferet el piha: 'And he said, This is Wickedness. And he thrust her back into the basket, and thrust down the leaden weight over its opening.' The angel's forceful action: she tries to get out; he forces her back in and seals the lid. Wickedness personified is contained and controlled — it will not escape from the container in which YHWH has confined it. The sealing of wickedness in the ephah anticipates the binding and sealing of evil in Revelation (Revelation 20:2-3: he seized the dragon and bound him for a thousand years).
9: vaessa et einai vaere vehineh shtei nashim yotz'ot veruach bekanfeihen ulahen kanafayim kekanfei hahasidah vativnah et ha'eifah bein ha'aretz uvein hashamayim: 'Then I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, two women coming forward! The wind was in their wings. They had wings like the wings of a stork, and they lifted up the basket between earth and sky.' Two women with stork wings (kekanfei hahasidah) transport the ephah. The hasidah (H2624, stork — from chesed, associated with faithful/loyal birds) has large, powerful wings suitable for long-distance flight. The ephah is lifted between earth and sky — in transit from the land of Israel to its destination.
10: vaomar el hamalakh haddover bi ana hemah molichot et ha'eifah vayomer elai livnot lah bayit be'eretz Shin'ar vehukhhan venuchah sham al mekhonatah: 'Then I said to the angel who talked with me, Where are they taking the basket? He said to me, To the land of Shinar, to build a house for it. And when this is prepared, they will set the basket down there on its base.' Eretz Shin'ar (the land of Shinar, H8152 — Genesis's name for Babylonia, Genesis 10:10; Zechariah 11:2) — wickedness is being sent back to Babylon, its spiritual home, where it will have its permanent dwelling (mekhonatah = its base, its established place). The removal of wickedness from the land of Israel to Shinar is the reversal of Ezra 1:7-11: the vessels taken from Jerusalem to Babylon are returned; now wickedness goes the other direction, to Babylon where it belongs. The purification of the land is accomplished by this cosmic removal.
11: vayomer elai livnot lah bayit be'eretz Shin'ar: See the previous verse (v10) for the full comment on the Shinar destination. The building of a house for Wickedness in Shinar represents the institutionalization and containment of evil in its appropriate domain — away from the covenant land. The eschatological vision: the holy land purified, wickedness exiled to its proper place.
